video_title: The $2 Trillion Problem Killing US Competitiveness
transcript: [music] >> Welcome to a special series of the Meb Faber Show on the past, [music] present, and future of America. I'm sitting down with some of the most notable historians, thinkers, and investors in US financial [music] history, all tied to my new coffee table book, Investing in America, The Rise of a 250-Year Bull Market, out July 4th. >> Meb Faber is the co-founder and chief investment officer at Cambria Investment Management. Due to industry regulations, he will not discuss any [music] of Cambria's sponsors on this podcast. All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinions and do not reflect the opinion of Cambria Investment Management [music] or its affiliates. For more information, visit cambriainvestments.com. >> Hey, hey, everybody. My guest today is Rebecca Anderson, senior fellow at the McKinsey Global Institute, McKinsey's business and economics research arm. She leads research on economic growth, financial system in the United States and globally. Earlier this year, she published one of my favorite pieces of the entire year, an amazing report titled At 250, Sustaining America's Competitive Edge. Rebecca, welcome to the show. >> Thank you for having me, Meb. >> I wanted to mention real quick, listeners, nothing in this episode is investing advice, which is always true, but particularly true today. We'll start a little bit broad as we look back at this period. It seems like there's almost been like a secret sauce, and so I'd love to hear what you think that might have been. >> Perhaps before answering that question, maybe also to just share a little bit about the impetus for why we did this report. Of course, as you mentioned, this is the 250th anniversary of the United States, and at the McKinsey Global Institute, we research deep structural forces that are changing in the global economy, and one of our big themes of research recently has been that we are at the cusp of a new era for the global economy. That's really largely defined by things like heightening geopolitical fragmentation, as well as rapidly advancing technology like AI. And so, we figured, given that we're at this juncture point in the global economy, and given the fact that the US has this big birthday or anniversary this year, it would be a really great time to take a step back and think about the world's largest economy. Can the US stay that way? And what would it take to go forward? And to your question, what has been that secret sauce, so to speak, for the last 250 years? Fundamentally, there are a couple foundations that the US has had throughout the last 250 years that have really shown through time and again. The first one that we mentioned is basically natural endowments. So, the US has had and continues to have tremendous natural resources. And different resources have been important at different moments in history. For example, in the first century, the fact that the United States had a ton of arable land, the most arable land per capita of any advanced economy or major economy today. Similarly, coal and oil were a really big part of the story in terms of just pure fossil fuels. Having copper reserves was really important for electrification. But then, in addition to that, geographic features of the country. For example, having a ton of navigable waterways internally, as well as access to deep water ports. The US has the most natural deep water ports out of any major economy in the world today. And in addition to that, the US has had the benefit of having geographic isolation in times when it's really mattered, such as World War II. So, there's all of these natural and geographic features that have, time and again, come to benefit the country. In addition to that natural set of advantages, there's also this idea of this culture of innovation and entrepreneurship that has come through time and again. By our accounting, US had some hand in three quarters of the top 100 inventions of the past 250 years. The US has been able to fundamentally reinvent its economy as time has gone on as well. Tied to both of these forces of the natural advantages as well as this culture of entrepreneurship, importantly, the US has also created the infrastructure and the institutions that have been able to harness both of those things. So, for example, we have a ton of navigable waterways, and we invented steamboats and the canal infrastructure to actually be able to harness the availability of these natural waterways. We had all this coal, but we couldn't transport coal to factories until railroads were developed on mass. So, the US historically, through much of its history, was a leader in infrastructure. That was really able to harness many of these natural features. On the institutional side, of course, we have the Constitution. The US Constitution has stood the test of time. It's the longest standing constitution still in effect today by major country, and it includes things like property rights, especially intellectual property rights that were able to nurture this sense of entrepreneurship. And, importantly, also from early on created this free trade within US states. So, one state could not tariff another state, for example. And this really helped develop this common market that allowed businesses to reach these economies of scale and harness the power of this really, really big internal market. So, it's been the foundations of this natural advantage and this culture of entrepreneurship, but then in addition to that, that was harnessed by the infrastructure and the institutions that actually supported them. >> All right, the US has been crushing it, world's largest economy. How did we get here? All luck? >> One really central element of of all of US history has been its capacity to reinvent itself over time. If we look back at 250 years of US history, we outline four historical chapters, as we call them. Each of them was defined by the US leading in some major either industry or segment of the global economy, but it was also defined by these new wellsprings of innovation and new thinking and technologies coming out. So, if we walk through those, the first one was really most of the first century of the United States. The US economy in that first century was largely defined by agriculture. Things like arable land were a critical resource as well as those internal navigable waterways, but the US fundamentally was an agricultural economy. Its main exports to the rest of the world were agricultural staples. However, as this was happening throughout the 19th century, there was a uniquely American, it was literally called the US system of manufacturing, that was being developed. So, you had companies like the Boston Manufacturing Company that was created in the early 1800s and multiple other manufacturing firms that really started to build up this unique system of manufacturing that was really based off of interchangeable parts. And a lot of it was because of lack of resources in some cases to other different types of materials and so on, but they had to be crafty about how exactly they built things. So, there was this unique system and by the 1850s even the US was noted for there's this Crystal Palace exhibition of in the 1850s where the US manufacturing is put on display and have lauded by many others from around the world. So, there was this S-curve to use a very consultant-y term there, but this manufacturing growth that was taking place in this first historical chapter that then really set up the US for success in that second chapter, which is really the US Industrial Revolution that took place post-Civil War. It was during this era within the time frame between the US Civil War and about World War II that the US became the world's leading economy and world leader in industry in terms of industrial and manufacturing output so on. There were also important developments taking place during this era related to technology. So, many crucial critical inventions like the electric lightbulb, also process innovations like the assembly line, were created during this period. And all of that really helped set up or tee up the US for success in the third historical chapter post-World War II. That was really defined by US leadership in science and technology. And it was during that era where there was the computing revolution, the microprocessor, the early PCs that were invented in the decades following World War II. And all of that then of course sets up the US for success in what the fourth chapter is, which is kind of where we're concluding that chapter now of this US digital services revolution and leadership. Technology is really front center, of course, internet internet applications, and so on, but also the role of finance. Finance is an interesting one because it is ever-present throughout so many of these eras. Even in the first era, the US was really building up this world-class financial sector. Even by the 1830s, had a financial sector that was competing with London in many ways. But, of course, the financial sector really grew in size and scope in that fourth historical chapter from the 1990s onward. Between each of these historical chapters, there was a major geopolitical event that happened. So, after the first one, it was the US Civil War. And between the second and the third one, it was World War II. And between the third and the fourth, it was the end of the Cold War. And at each of these points, there were also new technologies coming into the fold that were also shaping things. So, given the moment that we're in now, it's this natural point of, well, what has been happening over the last decade or so that appears to be teeing us up for the next era, and what the next historical chapter will be really defined by. And of course, AI is one really obvious one there, but I think time will tell as well exactly how the American economy will be defined in the coming decades in this next historical chapter. >> It's like a perfect storm of inputs and factors that really mattered. The interesting to me is just the sustainability. It feels like a little bit something in the water where, I mean, you look through some of these stats and we have like four pages of really cool statistics from this piece. Just how much above its weight the US punches. 5% of global population, that's tiny as far as the actual amount of people, but a quarter of GDP, 60% of top 100 firms by market cap, on and on, AI models, R&D expenditure. You mentioned 75 of the top inventions. It's kind of incredible. And one of the interesting things to me, too, is you mentioned the economy of today looks nothing like the economy of 50, even 100 years ago, which looks nothing like the economy of 200 years ago. You guys talk about this labor force dynamism and how workers reallocate, which is clearly a concern for a lot of people today is AI. A big topic on TV every day is AI is going to take over all the jobs. And thinking about, you know, a really interesting example in my mind is the manufacturing in the US, which used to be a huge portion of employment, but got pretty disrupted by globalization and other issues. Maybe talk a little bit about this labor force in general. And are we at a crossroads where we are currently with AI and some of the tech transitions currently? >> Of course, as you mentioned, one of the defining features of the US economy throughout the vast majority of its history, if not its full history, has been this point around labor force dynamism. And what we mean by that is effectively if a company falls under, the labor very quickly reallocates to a new industry or a new company. And importantly, it's not just within industries, it's across industries. So, the US labor force over time has shown tremendous capacity to basically reshuffle itself across where there are new opportunities in that stem. And the one important element of that throughout history that some economists have shown is the US educational system was a big part of that. US large-scale public education that expanded into high school throughout the 20th century played a big role in having and really nurturing that labor force historically. And then in addition to that, our general education type of system as opposed to forcing or pushing younger people into more apprenticeship type roles from a very early age, which is commonplace in a lot of other major economies, instead focusing on general education and problem-solving that has proven a successful model for giving workers a toolkit that they can pull from to more seamlessly move between industries over time. Of course, to your point, the last 30 years or so, especially in the US, in which in our work we call the fourth historical chapter, that has been a time of pretty great upheaval in that the manufacturing share of the economy has dropped significantly. It's today, I believe, just under 10% of the total employment, but it was much higher than that 50, even 30 years ago. And what happened as the manufacturing share of the economy declined is that all of the would-be workers in manufacturing either were able to make the jump to a higher-skilled, more services-based job or had to move to lower-skill, lower-wage service economy jobs. So, as we shifted from a manufacturing economy to a service economy, there was this bifurcation where there was a shift to this higher-end services and lower-end services. And what that has translated to is a much, much lower growth of both jobs and wages in the middle of the income distribution. So, of course, that is basically brought our economy to where it is today. And what's interesting about AI in particular is that now, whereas previous automation technologies were really associated in large part with manufacturing type jobs or jobs more in the middle of that distribution. Now, there's a question about whether AI is going to impact the higher-end services segment of basically income and jobs distribution. It's an open question exactly how, of course, this will play out. What some other MGI research had found is that about 57% of current hours that are worked could potentially be automated by today's technologies, many of which are in more of those high-skilled services type jobs. But, that doesn't mean that those jobs are necessarily going to disappear. There could very well be a complete reconfiguration of the way work is done. So, there could be new jobs that come up, like data science, for example. Data scientists today replaced a whole host of professions that used to exist, like human calculators, for example. So, there could be all sorts of reconfiguration of work, but there's a big question of how exactly that'll play out. What is clear, though, is that both businesses and community leaders, government leaders, need to start planning for this now. So, they need to start thinking through, how am I adopting AI as a business, and then how does my organization, and how do the roles need to reconfigure as a result of that? Correspondingly, how do we train up people to actually take those roles? All in all, US economy or US workforce has shown tremendous capacity to basically pivot where needed. At the same time, in the long stretches of time, it's not always immediate. In general, technology often comes with this complementarity to workers and creates like new types of jobs and work. >> Specifically on the manufacturing ramp-up, could you touch on that a little more? >> The US imported about $3 trillion worth of manufactured goods last year and about 25% of those we call Achilles heels in that they have at least two of these three trade dependencies that I outlined, meaning they're critical to national security, they're from a concentrated set of suppliers, or they are from a geopolitically distant trading partner. And what we've done in our latest research on this is we've calculated at a granular product level across 5,000 products through about 350 industries within manufacturing, what is the extent of US domestic production capacity? And then how does that compare to the amount of imports currently coming into the US? So if we look across all products, the our ramp up factor so to speak is a ramp up factor 1.3. In other words, in order to ramp up manufacturing capacity to basically offset imports as a whole, it would have to grow by about 30%. But what we're really focusing on this is those most critical trade dependencies. And if we look at the products that are facing these critical trade dependencies, that 25% of Achilles heels as we call them, manufacturing capacity would need to about double on average. And for about half of those products, it would need to more than quintuple. >> Are there examples just so people like might have an idea of what we're talking about? >> Certain forms of electronics like laptops, smartphones, server equipment for data centers. These are all things that the US currently produces very, very little of, but they are critical to the basic economic functioning, they're from very few supplying countries, and they are largely from geopolitically distant trading partners. So if the US were to say, we want to be able to make all of these critical things ourselves, then current capacity would have to, for those products in particular, actually grow more than tenfold. So it's a big, substantial jump up from what we have today. And in terms of the capital investment that would be needed to ramp up production specifically for those Achilles heel goods in the US, we estimate it to be about $2 trillion. And that would be encompassing not just the final step in production, but also all of the upstream manufacturing components of the supply chain. So, for laptops, for example, just doing the laptop assembly in the United States would not really do much in the way of eliminating these critical trade dependencies. You'd also need to, of course, have the semiconductors that go into the laptops and any kind of upstream materials that go into the semiconductors as well produced here. So, all in all, it would be about $2 trillion capital investment. And, of course, none of that would happen without a very clear business case. There's examples in the past like shale gas, for example. So, shale gas between 2005 to 2025, all the upstream elements of the shale gas industry, all of that investment was roughly equal to about $2 trillion. So, we've done this before, at least over a stretch of time. But, you know, that's a pretty substantial amount of investment if we think about the capital stock of the country today, it's about 3.7 trillion specifically for manufacturing. So, that's about a 50% jump up from where we are today and a pretty dramatic reconfiguration of the US industrial base. >> One of the things about the success story that's been the US is, if you look at the most billionaires, the most amount of wealthy people, there's the famous stat recently where everyone was talking about Mississippi the lowest per capita wealth or income, I don't even know which one they were doing in the US was higher than the UK, which was causing all sorts of people mental meltdowns, but there's no question the US the top Americans earn way more than their peers in Europe or Canada. You guys have the stat. But the bigger question is this divide between the top and the bottom, which is seemingly makes headlines every day now. And if you look at another stat you guys had was for the bottom half of Americans more income growth has come from government benefit than from wages. Has this been a normal feature of the past 200 years where there's been kind of this spread and stress across economic classes, or is this something that's particularly new in this modern world of giant multi-trillion dollar companies and where we are today? >> Fundamentally the dynamic that we've been seeing of the top and the bottom having this pretty significant growth in wages, but the middle seeing a very very slow growth. That is really a story that is fairly unique and confined to the last I would say 50 or so years in the economy. It's really moving in tandem with this shift from manufacturing to services. There is kind of one big era of US history I would say and that was this era I think economists historians debate exactly when it started, but I think everyone agrees in the stretch of time from about World War II to the mid or so 1970s was a time when there was this big big expansion in the middle class. And there was all sorts of reasons behind that. It was a boom time for the economy overall. The US was very much so still the world's largest manufacturer and really leading across industries. And at the same time of course the US was also coming out of World War II the US was about half of total global economic output. It was a very very different time for the economy. What's interesting then is that education was still expanding in terms of educational attainment. It was growing over time in those few decades coming out of World War II. And then in addition to that, any kind of technology developments were really labor complementing very very directly. There was also just less in the way of offshoring production and so on. That wave of things hadn't really happened yet when we're talking about these few decades. It was kind of this cascade of a ton of people all of a sudden getting education. The GI Bill was a big thing coming out of World War II and so on. And then the pace of technology growth at that time was also very labor complementing. So that was a time of dramatic middle-class expansion. If you go back in time prior to that, of course, before talking about the late 19th century, that was a time when basically inequality was growing. That was a time when it was the age of the robber barons, of the trusts, and so on. There hadn't been this big movement, there was pro-labor movement yet at that point in time. Of course, that tide changes a bit in the early 20th century with the progressive movement. And of course then at that point you have other important innovations like Henry Ford, the assembly line, and so on that really changed the nature of labor and how labor interacts with business and industry. That's all to say that it's ebbed and flowed over time. That if you look at any kind of chart of inequality over a long stretch of time in the United States, it's gone up and down and up and down over time. And right now we're in the trajectory of going back up. And we've been going back up now for several decades, but this is not the first time we've seen an upward trajectory in that. We'll see what AI in the next era brings on that front, but there's a swing that's happened over the history. >> There's a thought process from a book called The End of Times where they were I think it's The End of Times where they were talking about the supply and demand issue where it's just been a great situation where the middle class has shrunk because a lot of them have graduated to the upper class, but the problem that becomes is there's too many upper class relative like it's like almost like a balance of supply which I thought was really interesting way to frame it that I hadn't heard before. As you think about how amazing this run has been, it certainly hasn't been without cracks. If you look at some of y'all's stats on the physical constraints, you know, when government and this country has gotten to be so big, you see some kind of sludge and just inefficiencies. A lot of this has to do with infrastructure. There was one where y'all were talking about permitting for transmission projects takes over 6 and 1/2 years on average. There's over 650 energy projects that are stuck in the federal approval queue. And about half of US bridges over 50 years old. I feel like everyone's heard that for years and years now and they're like, "Well, when when are we ever going to fix them?" And port congestion costs the economy 100 billion annually. My goodness, that seems like a really big number. Talk to us a little bit about the struggles there. Is that something that's inevitable? Is it unique to the US? Is it something that's easily fixable that just happens to be kind of a governance problem? How do y'all think about these physical constraints? >> One thing that we mentioned in our paper is that indeed a number of what we would consider to be historical strengths are now becoming liabilities for our economy. Infrastructure, as you mentioned, is one of several and educational attainment is another one. We have national debt that is now at the levels it was coming out of World War II, relative to GDP. And then, of course, there's all of the harmful side effects that come from a loss of manufacturing capacity over the last couple decades. All of these things are headwinds, if we're thinking about the future of American economic competitiveness. On the infrastructure point, all the stats you just mentioned, of course, are things that we highlight or connected to things that we highlight in our paper. But, it is important to emphasize that historically, the US actually was a leader in infrastructure in terms of railroads, if we think about the late 19th century, the Eisenhower Interstate Highway System that was a major build-out in the 1950s. There's all sorts of examples historically of when the US was able to move very quickly and be a true leader in infrastructure by global standards. And it is, of course, this interesting feature of our country that we have multiple layers of government. In many ways, a federalist system of government has been a tremendous source of advantage for us as a country. It's because of this state, local, federal system, it means that states and local governments have capacity to innovate on things and experiment with things. And that's been a large part of the US success story. However, the flip side to that, of course, is that there are multiple layers of regulation to go through, multiple layers of permitting and such to get approval for a new infrastructure or energy project. And now, after decades of more and more regulation and permitting processes being layered on top of each other, now we have the system where potentially up to a 1.5 trillion dollars worth of projects are stuck in federal permitting at the moment. One comparison that's often made in this context is of course China and China like as being the next largest economy in the world that's rapidly advancing in technology and is itself now a true leader in terms of its infrastructure and so on. There's a lot of comparisons made of how long it takes to build a data center for example between the US and China. China I think made global headlines when during COVID it was able to build a hospital in the span of a couple days. It's pretty incredible how quickly things can get built in China but of course China also doesn't have the same system of regulations and and so on and and approval processes that we have in the United States and that's something that we're going to have to grapple with as a country if we want to be able to move quickly on things. So US China is one of the big ones of course with the of comparisons you can make on infrastructure. It's worth noting though that in other domains in terms of general competitiveness metrics around regulation and so on if you compare the US to Europe the US is able to move very quickly on many dimensions of things compared to Europe. Depending on what exact comparison you're making the US looks better or worse. >> Thing about China is there's just an immense size. You go through some of these stats. China produces 350,000 mechanical engineering graduates annually in the US it's 45,000. So literally like a 10x difference. Half the world's manufacturing output. You've seen certainly significant strides in both AI as well as particularly biotech. You've seen a lot of China overtaking US in clinical trials, clinical stage molecules which I think is fascinating. I wonder if you could expand even just a little more is this inevitable? >> China's so interesting to look at any of the economic trends or data. And of course, so much of China's story over the last couple decades has been deliberate choices made among the Chinese leadership to invest in specific industries and really develop this manufacturing sector to have partnerships where relevant or interesting for them with US or Western firms to try to get that knowledge diffusion going throughout China. So, there's quite a lot of manufacturing growth and know-how that's happened over the last several decades in China, and that's been in large part a very top-down, very deliberate process. And that's very different from a US model, where in the US, things by and large have happened organically or because there's a very clear business case that's associated with that. Just in terms of we look at how China's economy has evolved over time, it's been the story of very deliberate actions and very clear movements up the value chain, as you mentioned. So, now competing in many frontier technologies and frontier biotech and so on that China's been able to do. And there's a common thought that China's been really good at taking an idea from elsewhere and then just rapidly scaling it. But we do see that there are many domains where China is directly competing now, even at that top frontier side of different technologies. All that said though, if we think about things like AI and robotics and so on, the US and China have quite different strategies right now when it comes to AI. So, the US, it's really about getting to those frontier models of getting to AGI, let's say. And we see from the data in this report that 51% of the world's top models are from the United States. If we then look at China though and see where China is really shining, China leads by far the world in robotics and embedded AI systems. There's almost like two different games being played, so to speak. And there's a question of can these two systems coexist in harmony and so on. That is a major question that I think we'll see more and more about in the coming decade or so, but if the US wants to be competitive and regain any kind of manufacturing sector and many of these critical goods that it needs for national security, let's say, the US will also need to be investing in things like embedded AI and robotic systems. There's I think some things where the US can learn from the Chinese example, but of course, there's also just fundamentally different games being played here. >> It's fascinating to look at like kind of the different government models, also the public private. There seems to be a lot of push-pull right now between commentators, politicians, billionaires. It's obviously elections going on, so anytime that's happening that centers the discourse, but if you look back at the history of the US and the size of the government, how government taxed, different roles of government, it was totally different for probably the first what? 100 years, even really in then the next 50 years is kind of a transition period. This is me talking, totally disagree with me if that's not true, but looking back is like what percentage of GDP was government spending? And even the beginning of the 20th century was like low single digits, I think. And then like taxation as a percentage of GDP usually floats right around 20% regardless of how we tax, it seems to kind of just not change that much, but have we found homeostasis? Is it a situation that causes consistent issues, problems? Are there better ideas out there anyways to think about it as we get bombarded with these headlines about all these topics of government and taxes and spending and systems every day. >> So, a couple things I would say in terms of government spending over time, indeed the US if you were to get government spending as a share of GDP, it was quite small relative to GDP up until about the Great Depression. And then once the Great Depression hit, we saw this really dramatic upswing in terms of spending as a share of GDP. And that's continued to remain high since then. So, there was really this tectonic shift that came about with the New Deal and other programs of the 1930s Great Depression. Where it gets us to today, and of course spending is only one side of the story. Of course, there's taxation as well on the other side and then any kind of revenue streams that come about. There've been some significant shifts by the way when it comes to forms of taxation. Income taxes weren't really a big thing until the 20th century, but at the same time we had tariffs that were quite high throughout the 19th century. So, the nature of the revenue streams of government also changed quite a bit over time over the last 250 years. In any case, where we are today of course is that we do have this national debt as a share of GDP that's about 120% or so of GDP. That's back to the levels it was coming out of World War II. Because we're also in this environment now with very high interest rates, or structurally higher interest rates I should say, compared to the 2010s or or so on. That means that a greater and greater share of federal spending needs to be spent on interest payments on the debt. If we compare interest payments in just pure dollar terms to overall defense spending in 2024 was the first year that interest payments on the debt exceeded defense spending and it appears that that's only going to continue to widen that gap there. There's also of course this question of demand for treasuries on the market if there's more and more treasuries that will need to be put on the market in order to service the debt and raise capital. There's a real question of whether or not there will be sustained markets for that in the long term looking forward. If there just isn't the demand for the treasuries because of things like loss of confidence in the US government say then of course there will be much much higher interest rates that are faced by businesses so higher overall cost of capital and the US government would have much less fiscal space to respond to a crisis. Should one arise. >> Well, you're located there in DC so you got a local pulse on this and you go down to Capitol Hill you go to Monocle Butterworth. I don't know what's in DC these days near the White House old Ebbitt Grill maybe you go down there and you're sitting you're schmoozing having some wine chatting with some people and let's say a bunch of heavy hitter politicians call you over and they say all right Rebecca we heard you on the Meb Faber Show. It was awesome. We want to improve and continue some of the themes that you talk about in your paper. We want to ensure that the US is still a dominant force in the next 50 years particularly the next 10 and we're going to get it done. Where could we start to make some impactful policy ideas decisions? What could we be thinking about? Just you got 5 minutes to talk to us while we're waiting on dessert. What do you got for us? What would you say on the spot right there? >> So, I would point to the five prerequisites that we outlined in this report for what we would call sustained economic competitiveness. Good looking forward, given the fact that we're entering this new era of greater geopolitical fragmentation, as well as rapidly advancing AI. The incoming premise for it is that the US to be competitive needs to be able to compete in many of these frontier technologies. So, what do we need to actually be competitive in this? What do businesses, for example, need from government in order for this to happen? The first thing is a labor force. The labor force that is sufficiently skilled and dynamic in things like AI. There's, of course, the historical fact that we had talked about of US labor force dynamism. Over time, it's actually been going down slightly in recent years. That's connected to the manufacturing decline and shift bifurcation into services. But, fundamentally, 57% of current hours could be automated, and work is going to reconfigure pretty dramatically as a result of AI. So, workers need to be able to take roles as they are created. That means collaborations between business and government on large-scale upskilling efforts. It means new pushes to study certain trades that are in demand, especially for things like construction-related work or skilled trades like welding, let's say. It also means for students going into universities, a big push to study engineering to help fill those pipelines of in-demand jobs that will be there in the coming decades. So, there's this big labor force dimension or this big prerequisite that we need an AI fluent workforce. The second one is around capital that we need sustained long-term investment and a big part of this is related to US's ability to raise capital in the future and that's connected to the national debt, greater payments, greater interest payments debt in the face of higher interest rates looking forward. There's reason to believe that interest rates may remain structurally high if we look at the flows of savings in the world today and and the demand for capital. So, with tons of investment taking place for example at data centers, an estimated 3 trillion of investment in data centers coming up in the next 5 years alone. That's 10% of US GDP. There's a ton of investment that needs to take place, but at the same time the pool of savings in the world given certain trends related to demographics, the slowing of emerging market growth, these things appear that they will push down the pool of savings. All together, what that means is that the cost of capital or interest rates grows. So, there's this structurally higher interest rate dynamic at play here given the US fiscal situation that is something that we'll have to grapple with and has a channel that directly impacts businesses because US 10-year Treasury yields are really what inform market interest rates. And the third one I would say is on energy. There's an estimated 60% increase in the demand for energy expected through 2040. This is a jump up after several decades of pretty flat energy demand growth. So, that's a real big overhaul in the energy system that we're looking at. And that means that there will be a much greater demand for energy generation and transmission infrastructure developments. And so, there's the permitting process question again, as well as again the workforce question there. Related to that, broader infrastructure. So, infrastructure that connects goods and people and information. So, 20% of American homes right now don't have broadband access. If we're trying to think about the future where everyone is included in the economy, people need to be connected. And goods and information flows need to be able to meet each other and not be held up. And the very last point is around national economic security. In other words, supply chain resiliency. So, in 2025, the US imported about $3 trillion of manufactured goods. And of those, 25% faced two or three overlapping trade dependencies. And those trade dependencies were that they were from a geopolitically distant country, they're critical to national security, and they were from a small set of supplying countries. Overall, there's quite a lot of potential choke points in the global trading system there. And there's some real serious questions that businesses, but also government, need to ask about like what can be done to try to mitigate many of those dependencies. >> One other paper that you guys did that I loved, and I think y'all are doing an update, so you can give us a little preview, was this one on global balance sheets. Can you tell us a little bit about this paper? What was the idea, inspiration, and any updates y'all have? >> The inspiration or the thinking behind the global balance sheet series is that if you're a business, you don't just look at your P&L, your income statement, you also take your balance sheet into account to understand, to get a full picture of your economic health. And for national economies, especially government, that really isn't done. You know, of course, debt is a stock that everyone has in mind quite often, but there isn't as much of a holistic take of let's not just look at GDP, in other words, our income statement, let's look at the balance sheet, the actual assets and the full set of liabilities that correspond to that GDP. Looking at those two things together can actually give us a much better full picture of the economic health and wealth of both national economies as well as the global economy. One of the things that we do is we take, you know, more classic corporate finance ratios to compare how things trend over time. One of the big findings that we had was if we look at so many different asset classes, but collectively wealth against GDP, we see that wealth to GDP in many countries of the world, but especially the US where the data's really, really good, those two things in the long stretch of history of the last 100 years or so have largely calibrated to the same ratio over time. But, around the late '90s, that ratio started to climb. We're now at a place 25, 30 years in to this dynamic of growing balance sheets relative to GDP where these ratios are really high. You can almost think about it like a P/E ratio for the national economy. And if P/E ratios are really high, there's a few different questions that you can ask. Like, why is it high? If it's high now, does that mean that this is an expectation that earnings are going to catch up to that high price? Or is it a potential signal that that price might drop? And there might be an asset price correction of some kind. And so that's our intention of going into this. Let's look at how these ratios are playing out today and what they might tell us about the future. One really interesting thing about and what we're seeing now in the latest data that we're working on currently is that of course US equities are a big big part of the global story even. US equity markets are basically half of global market cap for publicly traded equities at least. And increasingly it appears that other economies' wealth is really increasingly shaped by US equity markets as well. With equity relative to GDP at all time highs, you know, nearly about double what it was or actually I believe triple what it was historically if you look at equity relative to GDP, it creates this question of how much longer can this go on? How much higher can this ratio go? It really creates this imperative for earnings to remain high or to actually grow to catch up with the high equity values. And there's a lot of wealth at stake fundamentally in that. >> I just joke with people. They're like, "How are we going to close this wealth gap?" I just I just have a nice bear market. Just nice fat bear market and that'll help. You know, it's fun. I'd seen some people do a US balance sheet and I think Ken Fisher and some others have done this and I'd never seen it globally. It's super cool, but the numbers get so big. This is 500 trillion. That's 500 trillion. And I just said the first time I read it, I was like, "Wait a minute. I can't even remember what comes after trillion." >> A [laughter] quadrillion. Yeah. >> Yeah, listeners, think about it for a second. Rebecca spoiled it, but I was like, "Hold on. Like, wait, what is it?" I don't remember it. But the numbers get so big it's hard to even comprehend on some of these. This might be a hard question for you, but you can try. As you kind of do all this research, was there one particular stat or it could be a factor or a story that really stood out for you as you were kind of going through any of these three research papers? The one to me, some of these things are just like boom, you see it and you're like wow. That explains a lot. I was looking at one where it said US VC investment is 10 times higher than Europe. And I was like, holy cow. Like it's not double, which already you'd be like wow, that's a lot. 10 times higher. And I was looking at the market cap of all these European companies that are in the AI space and I don't even think the biggest one was over 50 billion. And so it's just kind of just lays stark this just massive wide gap. Are there any ones that stick out in your mind or do you think are particularly jaw-dropping? >> There's so many, honestly. But if we're looking at the US history and what one of the big contributions that we hope others will appreciate from this US economic competitiveness work looking back on history is that as our calculation of the 75% of top inventions had some form of US involvement. Not all of them were led by an American, but there was some form of American involvement in at least 75% of the top 100. That should really shows the sheer power of US entrepreneurship, US ingenuity and for that matter US capital markets to be able to fund so many of these inventions as well. So that was one I would say really big one from a historical standpoint. If I'm thinking more recently, one thing that is quite interesting is of course US economy has a lot of challenges today. We mentioned the infrastructure, the the education and so on. But there's are many things that have gone really well for the US over the last decade or so, too. If you look at announced FDI inflows, greenfield inflows to the United States. >> What is FDI? >> Foreign direct investment. So, it's basically a multinational corporation, in large part, not always a multinational, but predominantly multinationals, that decide where to invest fund, basically where to build things, to be as direct as possible. And if we compare the period 2015 to 2019, and then we compare the period 2022 to 2025, the US announced FDI inflows for greenfield projects doubled during that time. That was more than any other region of the world. The US also, in that latter period 2022 to 2025, got the most FDI inflows out of any region of the world. A large portion of that was semiconductors, so of course, like big investments, like TSMC's investment, had a big role to play in that. Across all sorts of industries as well, not just semiconductors. So, the US, if you consider foreign direct investment voting with your feet type of thing of where multinationals are anticipating the growth and the opportunity to be, it's a really good leading indicator for that. So, trade and transactions that have occurred are basically a demonstration of the current state or what has happened in recent history already, but a leading indicator is these investment flows. So, that's one. The other one I would mention is if you look at labor productivity growth. The US in recent years has had booming labor productivity compared to where it was throughout the 2010s. The US has actually doubled relative to the 2010s, approximately, in its productivity growth. If you look at China, China's productivity growth rates are still higher than the US, but if you look at just the pure trajectory, the US is on a true productivity acceleration at least in recent years based off of that metric. Europe has been basically flat, more or less zero productivity growth over the last several years. So, it's an interesting dynamic at play here where for all of the challenges that we do have as an economy, things are also for at least certain pockets going really well. >> Get your act together, Europe. I don't know. But, it's funny something in the water. There's old Thomas Edison quote. I just I feel like there's no stigma, there's no shame about failure in the US, or at least it feels that way to me. I failed a lot. But, there's the old Edison quote where he's like, "I've not failed. I just found 10,000 ways that don't work." Which I think you go to a lot of countries and there is a little bit of a shame to failure or still a stigma to it. This has been super fun. I could talk to you for hours. As you have put some of these papers to bed as you're looking out for the rest of the year, 2026, walk around the mall in DC, going to the Air and Space, the National Gallery. What else you working on? What's on your brain for the rest of the year? >> So, of course, geopolitics is a big theme of our research now. So, increasingly thinking about the role of the dollar, what does a future financial system look like amid geopolitical fragmentation. But, in addition to that, but as I mentioned, this major growth of capital demand for things like major data centers, but also reindustrialization type projects, infrastructure projects, and so on. So, there's ton of capital demand coming into the system or that's expected to come into the system. But, at the same time, a real question about what's the pool of capital available if savings are actually declining or poised to decline at a global level. So, a big question about what that means for the financial system and what style or formation of a financial system is best placed for that. So, that's one really big overarching question that we're thinking about, of course. And then, in addition to that, continuing to work on this global balance sheet series and continuing to refine our thinking about that and putting out, hopefully in the next month or so, an annual report or what we hope to be an annual report on the global balance sheet, just a general update on that. There's quite a lot underway right now. >> Rebecca, people want to follow your work. They listen to this conversation, they say, "I love listening to her. I want to consume more of what she's up to." Is there a place to go to follow you? Easiest place? >> LinkedIn would be the best one. So, it's my name, Rebecca J. Anderson, at McKinsey Global Institute. That would be my best platform. >> We'll put a link in the show notes, listeners. Rebecca, it has been a blessing and a lot of fun talking to you today. Thanks so much for joining us. >> Wonderful. Well, thanks for having [music] me. >> Podcast listeners, we'll post show notes to today's conversation at mebfaber.com/podcast. If you love the show, if you hate it, shoot us feedback at the mebfabershow.com. We love to read the reviews. Please [music] review us on iTunes and subscribe to show anywhere good podcasts are found. Thanks for listening, friends, and good investing.

transcript_segments.text: [music]
>> Welcome to a special series of the Meb
Faber Show on the past, [music] present,
and future of America. I'm sitting down
with some of the most notable
historians, thinkers, and investors in
US financial [music] history, all tied
to my new coffee table book, Investing
in America, The Rise of a 250-Year Bull
Market, out July 4th.
>> Meb Faber is the co-founder and chief
investment officer at Cambria Investment
Management. Due to industry regulations,
he will not discuss any [music] of
Cambria's sponsors on this podcast. All
opinions expressed by podcast
participants are solely their own
opinions and do not reflect the opinion
of Cambria Investment Management [music]
or its affiliates. For more information,
visit cambriainvestments.com.
>> Hey, hey, everybody. My guest today is
Rebecca Anderson, senior fellow at the
McKinsey Global Institute, McKinsey's
business and economics research arm. She
leads research on economic growth,
financial system in the United States
and globally. Earlier this year, she
published one of my favorite pieces of
the entire year, an amazing report
titled At 250,
Sustaining America's Competitive Edge.
Rebecca, welcome to the show.
>> Thank you for having me, Meb.
>> I wanted to mention real quick,
listeners, nothing in this episode is
investing advice, which is always true,
but particularly true today. We'll start
a little bit broad as we look back at
this
period. It seems like there's almost
been like a secret sauce, and so I'd
love to hear what you think that might
have been.
>> Perhaps before answering that question,
maybe also to just share a little bit
about the impetus for why we did this
report. Of course, as you mentioned,
this is the 250th anniversary of the
United States, and at the McKinsey
Global Institute, we research deep
structural forces that are changing in
the global economy, and one of our big
themes of research recently has been
that we are at the cusp of a new era for
the global economy. That's really
largely defined by things like
heightening geopolitical fragmentation,
as well as rapidly advancing technology
like AI. And so, we figured, given that
we're at this juncture point in the
global economy, and given the fact that
the US has this big birthday or
anniversary this year, it would be a
really great time to take a step back
and think about the world's largest
economy. Can the US stay that way? And
what would it take to go forward? And to
your question, what has been that secret
sauce, so to speak, for the last 250
years? Fundamentally, there are a couple
foundations that the US has had
throughout the last 250 years that have
really shown through time and again. The
first one that we mentioned is basically
natural endowments. So, the US has had
and continues to have tremendous natural
resources. And different resources have
been important at different moments in
history. For example, in the first
century, the fact that the United States
had a ton of arable land, the most
arable land per capita of any advanced
economy or major economy today.
Similarly, coal and oil were a really
big part of the story in terms of just
pure fossil fuels. Having copper
reserves was really important for
electrification. But then, in addition
to that, geographic features of the
country. For example, having a ton of
navigable waterways internally, as well
as access to deep water ports. The US
has the most natural deep water ports
out of any major economy in the world
today. And in addition to that, the US
has had the benefit of having geographic
isolation in times when it's really
mattered, such as World War II. So,
there's all of these natural and
geographic features that have, time and
again, come to benefit the country. In
addition to that natural set of
advantages, there's also this idea of
this culture of innovation and
entrepreneurship
that has come through time and again. By
our accounting, US had some hand in
three quarters of the top 100 inventions
of the past 250 years. The US has been
able to fundamentally reinvent its
economy as time has gone on as well.
Tied to both of these forces of the
natural advantages as well as this
culture of entrepreneurship,
importantly, the US has also created the
infrastructure and the institutions that
have been able to harness both of those
things. So, for example, we have a ton
of navigable waterways, and we invented
steamboats and the canal infrastructure
to actually be able to harness the
availability of these natural waterways.
We had all this coal, but we couldn't
transport coal to factories until
railroads were developed on mass. So,
the US historically, through much of its
history, was a leader in infrastructure.
That was really able to harness many of
these natural features.
On the institutional side, of course, we
have the Constitution. The US
Constitution has stood the test of time.
It's the longest standing constitution
still in effect today by major country,
and it includes things like property
rights, especially intellectual property
rights that were able to nurture this
sense of entrepreneurship. And,
importantly, also from early on created
this free trade within US states. So,
one state could not tariff another
state, for example. And this really
helped develop this common market that
allowed businesses to reach these
economies of scale and harness the power
of this really, really big internal
market. So, it's been the foundations of
this natural advantage and this culture
of entrepreneurship, but then in
addition to that, that was harnessed by
the infrastructure and the institutions
that actually supported them.
>> All right, the US has been crushing it,
world's largest economy. How did we get
here?
All luck?
>> One really central element of of all of
US history has been its capacity to
reinvent itself over time. If we look
back at 250 years of US history, we
outline four historical chapters, as we
call them. Each of them was defined by
the US leading in some major either
industry or segment of the global
economy, but it was also defined
by these new wellsprings of innovation
and new thinking and technologies coming
out. So, if we walk through those, the
first one was really most of the first
century of the United States. The US
economy in that first century was
largely defined by agriculture. Things
like arable land were a critical
resource as well as those internal
navigable waterways, but the US
fundamentally was an agricultural
economy. Its main exports to the rest of
the world were agricultural staples.
However, as this was happening
throughout the 19th century, there was a
uniquely American, it was literally
called the US system of manufacturing,
that was being developed. So, you had
companies like the Boston Manufacturing
Company that was created in the early
1800s and multiple other manufacturing
firms that really started to build up
this unique system of manufacturing that
was really based off of interchangeable
parts. And a lot of it was because of
lack of resources in some cases to other
different types of materials and so on,
but they had to be crafty about how
exactly they built things. So, there was
this unique system and by the 1850s even
the US was noted for there's this
Crystal Palace exhibition of in the
1850s where the US manufacturing is put
on display and have lauded by many
others from around the world. So, there
was this S-curve to use a very
consultant-y term there, but this
manufacturing growth that was taking
place in this first historical chapter
that then really set up the US for
success in that second chapter, which is
really the US Industrial Revolution that
took place post-Civil War. It was during
this era within the time frame between
the US Civil War and about World War II
that the US became the world's leading
economy and world leader in industry in
terms of industrial and manufacturing
output so on. There were also important
developments taking place during this
era related to technology. So, many
crucial critical inventions like the
electric lightbulb, also process
innovations like the assembly line, were
created during this period. And all of
that really helped set up or tee up the
US for success in the third historical
chapter post-World War II. That was
really defined by US leadership in
science and technology. And it was
during that era where there was the
computing revolution, the
microprocessor, the early PCs that were
invented in the decades following World
War II. And all of that then of course
sets up the US for success in what the
fourth chapter is, which is kind of
where we're concluding that chapter now
of this US digital services revolution
and leadership. Technology is really
front center, of course, internet
internet applications, and so on, but
also the role of finance. Finance is an
interesting one because it is
ever-present throughout so many of these
eras. Even in the first era, the US was
really building up this world-class
financial sector. Even by the 1830s, had
a financial sector that was competing
with London in many ways. But, of
course, the financial sector really grew
in size and scope in that fourth
historical chapter from the 1990s
onward. Between each of these historical
chapters, there was a major geopolitical
event that happened. So, after the first
one, it was the US Civil War. And
between the second and the third one, it
was World War II. And between the third
and the fourth, it was the end of the
Cold War. And at each of these points,
there were also new technologies coming
into the fold that were also shaping
things. So, given the moment that we're
in now, it's this natural point of,
well, what has been happening over the
last decade or so that appears to be
teeing us up for the next era, and what
the next historical chapter will be
really defined by. And of course, AI is
one really obvious one there, but I
think time will tell as well exactly how
the American economy will be defined in
the coming decades in this next
historical chapter.
>> It's like a perfect storm of inputs and
factors that really mattered. The
interesting to me is just the
sustainability.
It feels like a little bit something in
the water where, I mean, you look
through some of these stats and we have
like four pages of really cool
statistics from this piece. Just how
much above its weight the US punches. 5%
of global population, that's tiny as far
as the actual amount of people, but a
quarter of GDP, 60% of top 100 firms by
market cap, on and on, AI models, R&D
expenditure. You mentioned 75 of the top
inventions. It's kind of incredible. And
one of the interesting things to me,
too, is you mentioned the economy of
today looks nothing like the economy of
50, even 100 years ago, which looks
nothing like the economy of 200 years
ago. You guys talk about this labor
force dynamism and how workers
reallocate, which is clearly a concern
for a lot of people today is AI. A big
topic on TV every day is AI is going to
take over all the jobs. And thinking
about, you know, a really interesting
example in my mind is the manufacturing
in the US, which used to be a huge
portion of employment, but got pretty
disrupted by globalization and other
issues. Maybe talk a little bit about
this labor force in general. And are we
at a crossroads where we are currently
with AI and some of the tech transitions
currently?
>> Of course, as you mentioned, one of the
defining features of the US economy
throughout the vast majority of its
history, if not its full history, has
been this point around labor force
dynamism. And what we mean by that is
effectively
if a company falls under, the labor very
quickly reallocates to a new industry or
a new company. And importantly, it's not
just within industries, it's across
industries. So, the US labor force over
time has shown tremendous capacity to
basically reshuffle itself across where
there are new opportunities in that
stem. And the one important element of
that throughout history that some
economists have shown is the US
educational system was a big part of
that. US large-scale public education
that expanded into high school
throughout the 20th century played a big
role in having and really nurturing that
labor force historically. And then in
addition to that, our general education
type of system as opposed to forcing or
pushing younger people into more
apprenticeship type roles from a very
early age, which is commonplace in a lot
of other major economies, instead
focusing on general education and
problem-solving that has proven a
successful model for giving workers a
toolkit that they can pull from to more
seamlessly move between industries over
time. Of course, to your point, the last
30 years or so, especially in the US, in
which in our work we call the fourth
historical chapter, that has been a time
of pretty great upheaval in that the
manufacturing share of the economy has
dropped significantly. It's today, I
believe, just under 10% of the total
employment, but it was much higher than
that 50, even 30 years ago. And what
happened as the manufacturing share of
the economy declined is that all of the
would-be workers in manufacturing
either were able to make the jump to a
higher-skilled, more services-based job
or had to move to lower-skill,
lower-wage service economy jobs. So, as
we shifted from a manufacturing economy
to a service economy, there was this
bifurcation where there was a shift to
this higher-end services and lower-end
services. And what that has translated
to is a much, much lower growth of both
jobs and wages in the middle of the
income distribution. So, of course, that
is basically brought our economy to
where it is today. And what's
interesting about AI in particular is
that now, whereas previous automation
technologies were really associated in
large part with manufacturing type jobs
or jobs more in the middle of that
distribution. Now, there's a question
about whether AI is going to impact the
higher-end services segment of basically
income and jobs distribution. It's an
open question exactly how, of course,
this will play out. What some other MGI
research had found is that about 57% of
current hours that are worked could
potentially be automated by today's
technologies, many of which are in more
of those high-skilled services type
jobs. But, that doesn't mean that those
jobs are necessarily going to disappear.
There could very well be a complete
reconfiguration of the way work is done.
So, there could be new jobs that come
up, like data science, for example. Data
scientists today replaced
a whole host of professions that used to
exist, like human calculators, for
example. So, there could be all sorts of
reconfiguration of work, but there's a
big question of how exactly that'll play
out. What is clear, though, is that both
businesses and community leaders,
government leaders, need to start
planning for this now.
So, they need to start thinking through,
how am I adopting AI as a business, and
then how does my organization, and how
do the roles need to reconfigure as a
result of that? Correspondingly, how do
we train up people to actually take
those roles? All in all, US economy or
US workforce has shown tremendous
capacity to basically pivot where
needed. At the same time, in the long
stretches of time, it's not always
immediate. In general, technology often
comes with this complementarity to
workers and creates like new types of
jobs and work.
>> Specifically on the manufacturing
ramp-up, could you touch on that a
little more?
>> The US imported about $3 trillion
worth of manufactured goods last year
and about 25% of those we call Achilles
heels in that they have at least two of
these three trade dependencies that I
outlined, meaning they're critical to
national security, they're from a
concentrated set of suppliers, or they
are from a geopolitically distant
trading partner. And what we've done in
our latest research on this is we've
calculated at a granular product level
across 5,000 products through about 350
industries within manufacturing, what is
the extent of US domestic production
capacity? And then how does that compare
to the amount of imports currently
coming into the US?
So if we look across all products, the
our ramp up factor so to speak is a ramp
up factor 1.3. In other words, in order
to ramp up manufacturing capacity to
basically offset imports as a whole, it
would have to grow by about 30%. But
what we're really focusing on this is
those most critical trade dependencies.
And if we look at the products that are
facing these critical trade
dependencies, that 25% of Achilles heels
as we call them,
manufacturing capacity would need to
about double on average. And for about
half of those products, it would need to
more than quintuple.
>> Are there examples just so people like
might have an idea of what we're talking
about?
>> Certain forms of electronics like
laptops, smartphones, server equipment
for data centers. These are all things
that the US currently produces very,
very little of, but they are critical to
the basic economic functioning, they're
from very few supplying countries, and
they are largely from geopolitically
distant trading partners. So if the US
were to say, we want to be able to make
all of these critical things ourselves,
then current capacity would have to, for
those products in particular, actually
grow more than tenfold. So it's a big,
substantial jump up from what we have
today. And in terms of the capital
investment that would be needed to ramp
up production specifically for those
Achilles heel goods in the US, we
estimate it to be about $2 trillion.
And that would be encompassing not just
the final step in production, but also
all of the upstream manufacturing
components of the supply chain. So, for
laptops, for example, just doing the
laptop assembly in the United States
would not really do much in the way of
eliminating these critical trade
dependencies. You'd also need to, of
course, have the semiconductors that go
into the laptops and any kind of
upstream materials that go into the
semiconductors as well produced here.
So, all in all, it would be about $2
trillion capital investment. And, of
course, none of that would happen
without a very clear business case.
There's examples in the past like shale
gas, for example. So, shale gas between
2005 to 2025, all the upstream elements
of the shale gas industry, all of that
investment was roughly equal to about $2
trillion. So, we've
done this before, at least over a
stretch of time. But, you know, that's a
pretty substantial amount of investment
if we think about the capital stock of
the country today, it's about 3.7
trillion specifically for manufacturing.
So, that's about a 50% jump up from
where we are today and a pretty dramatic
reconfiguration of the US industrial
base.
>> One of the things about the success
story that's been the US is, if you look
at the most billionaires, the most
amount of wealthy people, there's the
famous stat recently where everyone was
talking about Mississippi
the lowest per capita wealth or income,
